1:
"And Jacob called unto his sons, and said, Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days." The witnesses note Jacob gathering his sons to foretell their future in the last days. Gill notes Jacob called his sons, who were near at hand, or whom he sent for, to tell them what should befall them in the latter days. The witnesses note Jacob calling his sons together to tell them what should befall them in the last days; the solemn opening of the patriarch's prophetic blessing, the dying father gathering his sons to declare their future — that Jacob, near death, summons his sons for a prophetic word: "Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days"; the witnesses note the gathering, that Jacob "called unto his sons" and bade them "gather... together," that all the twelve might hear at once the words he was about to speak; the witnesses note the prophetic character of what follows, "that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days" — that Jacob, by the Spirit of God, would foretell the future of each son and his tribe, even to "the last days," reaching down through the generations and, in Judah's portion, to the days of Messiah; the witnesses note that this was no mere fatherly counsel but a prophecy, the aged patriarch made a prophet in his dying hour; the witnesses note the solemnity of a deathbed blessing, the last and weightiest words of the father of the tribes; so that Jacob gathers his sons for the prophetic blessing; that which shall befall you in the last days. The witnesses note And Jacob called unto his sons, and said, Gather yourselves together. The witnesses note that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days.

The witnesses note Jacob gathering his sons to foretell their future in the last days — no mere fatherly counsel but a prophecy, the dying patriarch made a prophet, reaching down the generations and, in Judah's portion, to the days of Messiah.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn opening of the prophetic blessing. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob, near death, gathered his sons to declare by the Spirit what should befall them in the last days, a true prophecy. The witnesses note the called his sons: "Jacob called unto his sons" — Gill notes summoned them; the called his sons; the summoned; the the twelve; the near; the gathered. The expositors observe the gather together: "Gather yourselves together" — all to hear at once; the gather together; the all; the at once; the together; the the words. The witnesses press the prophetic word: a prophetic word, not mere counsel; the prophetic word; the prophetic; the not counsel; the the Spirit; the foretold. The expositors draw the lesson on the last days: "in the last days" — down the generations; the last days; the the last days; the the generations; the the future; the each tribe. The witnesses observe the to Messiah: in Judah's portion, to Messiah; the to Messiah; the Judah; the Messiah; the the days; the reaching. The expositors note the a prophet: the patriarch made a prophet; the a prophet; the the patriarch; the a prophet; the dying; the his hour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And Jacob called unto his sons and said Gather yourselves together, the that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days, and the prophetic blessing. The verse thus records the opening of the prophetic blessing — teaching that Jacob gathered his sons to declare by the Spirit what should befall them in the last days.
2:
"Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob; and hearken unto Israel your father." The witnesses note Jacob's repeated call to gather and hearken. Keil and Delitzsch note the repeated summons, Gather yourselves together and hear, ye sons of Jacob, and hearken unto Israel your father. Gill notes this is repeated to hasten them, and to suggest he had something of importance to make known, which he chose to do when they were together. The witnesses note Jacob repeating the call to gather and hear, as sons of Jacob, and to hearken to Israel their father; the urgent and twofold summons to attend, marking the weight of the coming words — that Jacob doubles his call to fix their attention: "Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob; and hearken unto Israel your father"; the witnesses note the repetition, that Jacob says "gather yourselves together" again (v.1), to hasten them and to mark that he had a weighty thing to declare, fit to be heard by all at once; the witnesses note the double word for attention, "hear... and hearken," pressing them to give earnest heed to what their dying father should say; the witnesses note the two names the commentators mark, "ye sons of Jacob" and "Israel your father" — the natural name and the covenant name, that they should hear both as the children of Jacob and as the heirs of the Israel of God, to whom the promises belonged; the witnesses note the solemnity of the charge, fitting words that were prophecy and last testament both; so that Jacob calls his sons to earnest attention; and hearken unto Israel your father. The witnesses note Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob. The witnesses note and hearken unto Israel your father.

The witnesses note Jacob's twofold summons — "hear... and hearken" — pressing earnest heed for weighty words; naming them both "sons of Jacob" and heirs of "Israel your father," the natural name and the covenant name to whom the promises belonged.

So the witnesses make the verse the urgent summons to attend. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob repeated his call and pressed his sons to earnest heed, marking the weight of the prophetic words to come. The witnesses note the gather and hear: "Gather yourselves together, and hear" — Gill notes to hasten them; the gather and hear; the to hasten; the weighty; the at once; the gathered. The expositors observe the hear and hearken: "hear... and hearken" — earnest heed; the hear and hearken; the earnest heed; the attention; the give heed; the the words. The witnesses press the sons of Jacob: "ye sons of Jacob" — the natural name; the sons of Jacob; the the natural name; the the children; the of Jacob; the heard. The expositors draw the lesson on the Israel your father: "Israel your father" — the covenant name; the Israel your father; the the covenant name; the the promises; the the heirs; the of God. The witnesses observe the the weight: the weight of the coming words; the the weight; the weight; the prophecy; the testament; the solemn. The expositors note the both names: both names marked together; the both names; the both; the the natural; the the covenant; the marked. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Gather yourselves together and hear ye sons of Jacob, the and hearken unto Israel your father, and the summons. The verse thus records the urgent summons — teaching that Jacob pressed his sons to earnest heed, marking the weight of the prophetic words to come.
3:
"Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength, the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power:" The witnesses note Jacob owning Reuben as his firstborn, with all the dignity that belonged to it. Gill notes Jacob addressed Reuben first, owned him as his firstborn, did not cashier him from his family nor disinherit him, but acknowledged his natural pre-eminence. The witnesses note Jacob owning Reuben as his firstborn, his might and the beginning of his strength, the excellency of dignity and of power; the acknowledgment of Reuben's natural birthright dignity, before the sentence that follows — that Jacob first sets out what Reuben was by birth: "Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength, the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power"; the witnesses note the honour owned, that Reuben was Jacob's "firstborn," "the beginning of my strength" (the first fruit of his manhood), to whom by birth belonged "the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power" — the firstborn's threefold prerogative of the double portion, the priesthood, and the rule in the family; the witnesses note that Jacob does not deny Reuben his natural place as eldest, owning him fully and tenderly; the witnesses note that this acknowledgment makes the coming sentence the more solemn, that one born to such dignity should forfeit it; the witnesses note the setting-up of the height from which Reuben fell, that the next verse might show the fall; so that Jacob owns Reuben's birthright dignity; the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power. The witnesses note Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength. The witnesses note the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power.

The witnesses note Jacob owning Reuben as his firstborn — "the beginning of my strength" — to whom belonged the firstborn's threefold prerogative of dignity and power; setting up the height from which, by his sin, Reuben fell.

So the witnesses make the verse the acknowledgment of Reuben's birthright dignity. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob owned Reuben fully as his firstborn, with all the prerogative of the eldest, before the sentence of forfeiture. The witnesses note the my firstborn: "thou art my firstborn" — Gill notes owned, not disinherited; the my firstborn; the owned; the not disinherited; the the eldest; the acknowledged. The expositors observe the beginning of strength: "the beginning of my strength" — the first fruit; the beginning of strength; the the first fruit; the his manhood; the the beginning; the his might. The witnesses press the dignity and power: "the excellency of dignity, and... power" — the threefold prerogative; the dignity and power; the the prerogative; the the double portion; the the priesthood; the the rule. The expositors draw the lesson on the natural place: his natural place owned; the natural place; the his place; the the eldest; the owned; the tenderly. The witnesses observe the the height: the height from which he fell; the the height; the the height; the the fall; the forfeit; the the next verse. The expositors note the more solemn: makes the sentence more solemn; the more solemn; the solemn; the the sentence; the forfeit; the such dignity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Reuben thou art my firstborn my might and the beginning of my strength, the the excellency of dignity and the excellency of power, and the birthright dignity. The verse thus records the acknowledgment of Reuben's dignity — teaching that Jacob owned him fully as firstborn before the sentence of forfeiture.
4:
"Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it: he went up to my couch." The witnesses note the sentence on Reuben, that for his sin he should not excel. Gill notes this is not to be understood of mere levity of mind, but of the impetuous force of his lust breaking forth like water, carrying him into sin. The witnesses note Jacob declaring Reuben unstable as water and that he should not excel, because he defiled his father's bed; the solemn sentence of forfeited pre-eminence for Reuben's grievous sin — that Jacob pronounces the loss of the birthright: "Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it"; the witnesses note the figure, "Unstable as water" — that Reuben was like water, which boils up and overflows but has no firmness, his passions ungoverned, breaking forth in the impetuous lust that carried him into sin; the witnesses note the sentence, "thou shalt not excel" — that, for all his birthright dignity (v.3), Reuben should not have the pre-eminence, his tribe never rising to greatness or rule in Israel; the witnesses note the cause, "because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it" — the grievous sin of Reuben with Bilhah (35:22), by which he dishonoured his father and forfeited his place; the witnesses note the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave, the firstborn's honour passing from Reuben (the rule to Judah, the priesthood to Levi, the double portion to Joseph); so that Reuben forfeits the birthright for his sin; thou shalt not excel. The witnesses note Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel. The witnesses note because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it.

The witnesses note the sentence on Reuben — "Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel" — his ungoverned passion forfeiting the birthright for his sin with Bilhah; the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave, the firstborn's honour passing from him.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben's forfeited pre-eminence. The lesson the expositors draw is that Reuben, unstable as water, lost the birthright dignity for his grievous sin, the justice of God passing the firstborn's honour from him. The witnesses note the unstable as water: "Unstable as water" — Gill notes the impetuous lust; the unstable as water; the impetuous lust; the like water; the no firmness; the ungoverned. The expositors observe the shalt not excel: "thou shalt not excel" — no pre-eminence; the shalt not excel; the no pre-eminence; the not rise; the his tribe; the never. The witnesses press the the cause: "thou wentest up to thy father's bed" — the sin with Bilhah; the the cause; the the sin; the Bilhah; the his father's bed; the dishonoured. The expositors draw the lesson on the defiled it: "then defiledst thou it" — forfeited his place; the defiled it; the defiled; the forfeited; the his place; the the birthright. The witnesses observe the sin loses: sin loses what nature gave; the sin loses; the sin; the loses; the nature gave; the the honour. The expositors note the honour passed: the firstborn's honour passed to others; the honour passed; the the honour; the passed; the to Judah; the Levi and Joseph. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Unstable as water thou shalt not excel, the because thou wentest up to thy father's bed then defiledst thou it, and the forfeited birthright. The verse thus records Reuben's forfeited pre-eminence — teaching that Reuben lost the birthright for his sin, the justice of God passing the firstborn's honour from him.
5:
"Simeon and Levi are brethren; instruments of cruelty are in their habitations." The witnesses note Jacob joining Simeon and Levi as brethren in temper and in cruelty. Gill notes they are called brethren not merely as being so by father and mother, for so were others, but because they were of like tempers, dispositions, and conduct. The witnesses note Jacob calling Simeon and Levi brethren, with instruments of cruelty in their habitations; the joining of the two for their shared fierce and cruel temper, shown at Shechem — that Jacob marks them as one in their violence: "Simeon and Levi are brethren; instruments of cruelty are in their habitations"; the witnesses note the sense of "brethren" here, not the natural tie only (for all twelve were brethren), but that Simeon and Levi were "brethren" in temper, alike in their fierce, cruel, and revengeful disposition, confederate in evil; the witnesses note the charge, "instruments of cruelty are in their habitations" — that their swords (or their counsels and devices) were instruments of cruelty, pointing back to the bloody deed at Shechem, where they slew all the males in revenge for their sister Dinah (34:25-26); the witnesses note that Jacob, even on his deathbed, would not pass over so great a sin, but marks it for the warning of their tribes; the witnesses note the difference of this from a blessing, that for these two the father's word was reproof and sentence; so that Jacob joins Simeon and Levi for their cruelty; instruments of cruelty are in their habitations. The witnesses note Simeon and Levi are brethren. The witnesses note instruments of cruelty are in their habitations.

The witnesses note Jacob joining Simeon and Levi as "brethren" in fierce temper — their swords instruments of cruelty, pointing to the bloody deed at Shechem; the dying father not passing over so great a sin, but marking it for the warning of their tribes.

So the witnesses make the verse the joining of Simeon and Levi for their cruelty. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob marked Simeon and Levi as one in their fierce, cruel temper, shown at Shechem, even on his deathbed not passing over the sin. The witnesses note the are brethren: "Simeon and Levi are brethren" — Gill notes alike in temper; the are brethren; the alike; the temper; the disposition; the confederate. The expositors observe the not the natural tie: not the natural tie only; the not the natural tie; the not natural; the the tie; the all twelve; the in temper. The witnesses press the instruments of cruelty: "instruments of cruelty" — their swords; the instruments of cruelty; the their swords; the their counsels; the cruelty; the the devices. The expositors draw the lesson on the at Shechem: pointing to the deed at Shechem; the at Shechem; the Shechem; the the bloody deed; the the males; the slew. The witnesses observe the for Dinah: in revenge for Dinah; the for Dinah; the Dinah; the revenge; the their sister; the the deed. The expositors note the not passed over: the dying father not passing the sin over; the not passed over; the not passed; the the sin; the the warning; the the tribes. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Simeon and Levi are brethren, the instruments of cruelty are in their habitations, and the cruelty. The verse thus records the joining of Simeon and Levi — teaching that Jacob marked them as one in their fierce, cruel temper, shown at Shechem.
6:
"O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united: for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall." The witnesses note Jacob disavowing all part in the cruel counsel of Simeon and Levi. Gill notes Jacob said this as abhorring the wicked counsel they had taken of slaying the Shechemites, and lest any should think he had a hand in it. The witnesses note Jacob praying that his soul come not into their secret nor his honour be joined to their assembly, for in their anger they slew a man and in their selfwill digged down a wall; the patriarch's solemn disavowal of their bloody deed, clearing himself of all share in it — that Jacob abhors and renounces their counsel: "O my soul, come not thou into their secret... for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall"; the witnesses note the earnest disclaiming, "O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united" — that Jacob, abhorring their "secret" (their cruel counsel and conspiracy at Shechem), prays that he had no part in it, neither his "soul" nor his "honour" joined to so bloody a deed; the witnesses note the charge stated plainly, "in their anger they slew a man" (the men of Shechem) "and in their selfwill they digged down a wall" (or houghed oxen, broke down the city), a deed of fierce anger and stubborn self-will; the witnesses note that Jacob, far from owning the deed, here solemnly clears himself before God and his sons of any share in their cruelty; the witnesses note the abhorrence of a godly man for bloody violence done even in a kinsman's cause; so that Jacob disowns all part in their cruel deed; in their selfwill they digged down a wall. The witnesses note O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united. The witnesses note for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall.

The witnesses note Jacob's solemn disavowal — "O my soul, come not thou into their secret" — clearing himself of all share in the bloody deed at Shechem; the abhorrence of a godly man for fierce violence done even in a kinsman's cause.

So the witnesses make the verse Jacob's disavowal of their cruel counsel. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob solemnly disclaimed any part in the bloody deed of Simeon and Levi at Shechem, abhorring their fierce and self-willed violence. The witnesses note the come not into their secret: "come not thou into their secret" — Gill notes abhorring their counsel; the come not into their secret; the their counsel; the no part. The expositors observe the mine honour: "mine honour, be not... united" — no share in it; the mine honour; the not joined; the the deed. The witnesses press the slew a man: "in their anger they slew a man" — the men of Shechem; the slew a man; the Shechem; the their anger. The expositors draw the lesson on the digged down a wall: "in their selfwill they digged down a wall" — broke the city; the digged down a wall; the the city; the stubborn. The expositors note the abhorrence: the abhorrence of a godly man; the abhorrence; the godly; the bloody. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for in their anger they slew a man and in their selfwill they digged down a wall, and the disavowal. The verse thus records Jacob's disavowal — teaching that Jacob disclaimed any part in the bloody deed at Shechem, abhorring their violence.
7:
"Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel: I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel." The witnesses note Jacob cursing the anger of Simeon and Levi and foretelling their scattering. Gill notes their anger was sinful in its nature, criminal and detestable, strong, fierce, and furious in its operation, and so justly cursed; not the persons, but the sin. The witnesses note Jacob cursing their fierce anger and cruel wrath, and declaring he would divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel; the sentence on their sin and its strange fulfilment in the scattering of the two tribes — that Jacob curses the sin and foretells the punishment: "Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel: I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel"; the witnesses note the careful aim of the curse, "Cursed be their anger... and their wrath" — that Jacob curses not the persons of his sons but their "anger" and "wrath," the sinful passion itself, "fierce" and "cruel," justly accursed; the witnesses note the sentence, "I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel" — that the two tribes should be scattered and not have a settled, undivided inheritance of their own; the witnesses note the remarkable fulfilment the commentators trace — that Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled, while Levi received no land at all but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel (yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood); the witnesses note the truth that God can make even a curse the means of mercy, as in Levi; so that Jacob curses their wrath and foretells their scattering; I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel. The witnesses note Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel. The witnesses note I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel.

The witnesses note Jacob cursing their fierce anger — not the persons but the sin — and foretelling the scattering of the two tribes; fulfilled as Simeon dwindled within Judah and Levi received no land, yet Levi's scattering turned by grace to honour in the priesthood.

So the witnesses make the verse the curse on their wrath and the scattering foretold. The lesson the expositors draw is that Jacob cursed the sinful anger of Simeon and Levi and foretold their scattering, fulfilled in the two tribes, yet Levi's turned to honour. The witnesses note the cursed be their anger: "Cursed be their anger" — Gill notes the sin, not the persons; the cursed be their anger; the the sin; the not the persons; the the passion; the accursed. The expositors observe the fierce and cruel: "fierce... cruel" — justly cursed; the fierce and cruel; the justly; the the wrath. The witnesses press the divide and scatter: "I will divide... and scatter them" — no settled portion; the divide and scatter; the divide; the scatter; the no portion; the dispersed. The expositors draw the lesson on the Simeon dwindled: Simeon dwindled within Judah; the Simeon dwindled; the Simeon; the within Judah; the scattered; the the tribe. The witnesses observe the Levi no land: Levi received no land; the Levi no land; the Levi; the no land; the dispersed; the the cities. The expositors note the turned to honour: Levi's scattering turned to honour; the turned to honour; the the priesthood; the by grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel, and the curse and scattering. The verse thus records the curse and the scattering foretold — teaching that Jacob cursed their sinful anger and foretold their scattering, fulfilled in the two tribes, yet Levi's turned to honour.
8:
"Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise: thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies; thy father's children shall bow down before thee." The witnesses note the great blessing of Judah, praised by his brethren and victorious over his foes. Gill notes Judah's name signifies praise, given by his mother whose heart was filled with praises to God; he should be praised by his brethren for his virtues, and especially his famous seed the Messiah, of whom he was a type. The witnesses note Judah being praised by his brethren, his hand on the neck of his enemies, and his brethren bowing before him; the turn from sentence to blessing, the pre-eminence given to Judah and pointing to Christ — that Jacob exalts Judah above his brethren: "Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise: thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies; thy father's children shall bow down before thee"; the witnesses note the play on the name, "Judah" meaning "praise," that he should be "he whom thy brethren shall praise," honoured and esteemed for his virtues, his tribe famous for heroes like David; the witnesses note the victory promised, "thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies" — that Judah should subdue his foes, his hand on their neck in conquest; the witnesses note the rule given, "thy father's children shall bow down before thee" — that the other tribes (not Leah's sons only but all) should yield him pre-eminence and homage; the witnesses note the great type the commentators mark, that all this points beyond the tribe to Christ, the Lion of Judah, "whom his brethren praise," who subdues his enemies and before whom every knee shall bow; so that Judah is exalted, a type of the praised and conquering Christ; thy father's children shall bow down before thee. The witnesses note Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise; thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies. The witnesses note thy father's children shall bow down before thee.

The witnesses note Judah exalted above his brethren — praised, victorious, and given rule — pointing beyond the tribe to Christ, the Lion of Judah, whom his brethren praise, who subdues his enemies and before whom every knee shall bow.

So the witnesses make the verse the exaltation of Judah, a type of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Judah was given praise, victory, and rule over his brethren, pointing to Christ the Lion of Judah. The witnesses note the name praise: "Judah... whom thy brethren shall praise" — Gill notes the name means praise; the name praise; the the name; the means praise; the honoured; the esteemed. The expositors observe the famous tribe: his tribe famous for heroes; the famous tribe; the heroes; the David. The witnesses press the hand on the neck: "thy hand... in the neck of thine enemies" — victory; the hand on the neck; the victory; the subdue; the his foes; the conquest. The expositors draw the lesson on the bow down: "thy father's children shall bow down" — the tribes yield homage; the bow down; the homage; the the tribes; the yield; the pre-eminence. The witnesses observe the the Lion of Judah: the type of Christ, the Lion of Judah; the the Lion of Judah; the the Lion; the Christ; the the type; the his seed. The expositors note the every knee bow: before Christ every knee shall bow; the every knee bow; the every knee; the Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the thy father's children shall bow down before thee, and the exaltation. The verse thus records the exaltation of Judah — teaching that Judah was given praise, victory, and rule, pointing to Christ the Lion of Judah.
9:
"Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up: he stooped down, he couched as a lion, and as an old lion; who shall rouse him up?" The witnesses note Judah compared to a lion, strong and not to be roused. Keil and Delitzsch note Jacob compares Judah to a young lion, gone up from the prey, lying down like a lion, like a lioness whom none can rouse. Gill notes he was comparable to a young lion for his strength, courage, and generosity. The witnesses note Judah likened to a lion's whelp, gone up from the prey, couched as a lion whom none dare rouse; the figure of the lion that marks Judah's strength and dominion, and points to Christ the Lion of Judah — that Jacob sets forth Judah's lion-like majesty: "Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up: he stooped down, he couched as a lion... who shall rouse him up?"; the witnesses note the threefold image, the "lion's whelp" (the young lion, growing in strength), the lion "gone up from the prey" (returned victorious from the hunt), and the lion that has "couched" or "lain down" in such majesty and might that none dare "rouse him up"; the witnesses note that this figures Judah's growth from a young tribe to a strong and ruling one, courageous, victorious, and secure in his strength; the witnesses note the great Messianic reference the commentators draw, that the "Lion of the tribe of Judah" is Christ himself (Rev. 5:5), strong to conquer, who, having "gone up from the prey" of his finished work, reigns in unassailable majesty; the witnesses note the dignity of the figure, fit for both the tribe and its great Son; so that Judah is the lion, a type of the conquering Christ; who shall rouse him up? The witnesses note Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up. The witnesses note he couched as a lion, and as an old lion; who shall rouse him up?

The witnesses note Judah likened to a lion — young, victorious from the prey, couched in such majesty that none dare rouse him — figuring his strength and dominion, and pointing to the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Christ himself, who reigns in unassailable majesty.

So the witnesses make the verse Judah the lion, a type of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the lion figures Judah's strength and dominion, and above all Christ the Lion of Judah, strong to conquer and reigning in majesty. The witnesses note the lion's whelp: "Judah is a lion's whelp" — Gill notes strength and courage; the lion's whelp; the strength; the courage; the the young lion; the growing. The expositors observe the from the prey: "from the prey... thou art gone up" — victorious; the from the prey; the victorious; the the hunt; the gone up; the returned. The witnesses press the couched as a lion: "he couched as a lion" — in majesty; the couched as a lion; the lain down; the secure. The expositors draw the lesson on the none rouse: "who shall rouse him up?" — none dare; the none rouse; the none dare; the rouse; the the majesty; the unassailable. The witnesses observe the the Lion of Judah: the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Christ; the the Lion of Judah; the Christ; the conquer. The expositors note the reigns in majesty: Christ reigns in majesty; the reigns in majesty; the the finished work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he couched as a lion and as an old lion who shall rouse him up, and the lion. The verse thus records Judah the lion — teaching that the lion figures Judah's strength and dominion, and above all Christ the Lion of Judah.
10:
"The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come; and unto him shall the gathering of the people be." The witnesses note the great prophecy of the sceptre and the coming of Shiloh. Gill notes the sceptre signifies dominion, power, and authority; it should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, that is, the Messiah, to whom the gathering of the people should be. The witnesses note Jacob foretelling that the sceptre and lawgiver should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, and unto him the gathering of the people; the supreme Messianic prophecy of the chapter, the rule abiding in Judah until Christ — that Jacob speaks the great word of promise: "The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come; and unto him shall the gathering of the people be"; the witnesses note the meaning of "the sceptre" and "lawgiver," not the bare continuance of the tribe (for Benjamin and Levi also continued), but "dominion, power, and authority" — that rule and government should belong to Judah and not depart until a set time; the witnesses note the great term "Shiloh," which the commentators take for the Messiah (whether read as "he whose right it is," "the peaceable one," or "the sent"), that the sceptre should abide in Judah "until Shiloh come," that is, until the coming of Christ, who sprang from Judah; the witnesses note the fulfilment, that Judah held a distinct tribal and ruling standing until the time of Christ, and that with his coming the old polity passed; the witnesses note the gathering, "unto him shall the gathering of the people be" — that to Shiloh, the Messiah, the nations should be gathered, the Gentiles brought in; so that the prophecy fixes the rule in Judah until the coming of Christ; unto him shall the gathering of the people be. The witnesses note The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come. The witnesses note and unto him shall the gathering of the people be.

The witnesses note the supreme Messianic prophecy — the sceptre abiding in Judah "until Shiloh come" — taken by the commentators for the Messiah; rule and dominion belonging to Judah until Christ, to whom "the gathering of the people," the Gentiles, should be.

So the witnesses make the verse the great prophecy of Shiloh. The lesson the expositors draw is that dominion should abide in Judah until the coming of Shiloh, the Messiah, unto whom the gathering of the nations should be. The witnesses note the the sceptre: "The sceptre shall not depart from Judah" — Gill notes dominion and authority; the the sceptre; the dominion; the authority; the rule; the government. The expositors observe the not the bare tribe: not the bare tribe, but rule; the not the bare tribe; the but rule. The witnesses press the until Shiloh: "until Shiloh come" — the Messiah; the until Shiloh; the Shiloh; the the Messiah; the the peaceable; the the sent. The expositors draw the lesson on the abide until Christ: rule abides until Christ; the abide until Christ; the abide; the until Christ; the of Judah; the came. The witnesses observe the the old polity passed: with Christ the old polity passed; the the old polity passed; the his coming; the fulfilled. The expositors note the gathering of the people: "the gathering of the people" — the Gentiles brought in; the gathering of the people; the the people; the the Gentiles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and unto him shall the gathering of the people be, and the prophecy of Shiloh. The verse thus records the great prophecy of Shiloh — teaching that dominion should abide in Judah until the Messiah, unto whom the nations should be gathered.
11:
"Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine; he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes:" The witnesses note the figures of abundance, the vine and the wine, in Judah's portion. Gill notes this may signify that vines should grow in such plenty in Judah's land that a man might bind his ass to a vine, or it may be applied to Shiloh the Messiah. The witnesses note the binding of the foal to the vine and the washing of garments in wine and the blood of grapes; the figures of the rich plenty of Judah's land, applied also to Christ and his blood — that Jacob paints abundance under the vine and the wine: "Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine; he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes"; the witnesses note the first figure, "binding his foal unto the vine... unto the choice vine" — that Judah's land should so abound in vines, large and strong, that one might tie an ass to a vine as to a post, and not grieve if it ate of so plentiful a growth, a picture of great fruitfulness (and Judah's land, as Engedi, was famous for vines); the witnesses note the second figure, "he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes" — the grapes so plentiful that wine should be as common as water for washing, a hyperbole of abundance; the witnesses note the Messianic application the commentators draw, that "the blood of grapes" points to the blood of Christ (cf. Isa. 63:1-3), the true Vine, who treads the winepress and washes his garments in blood; the witnesses note the richness fit for both the fruitful tribe and its great Son; so that Judah's plenty is figured, with a glance at Christ's blood; he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes. The witnesses note Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine. The witnesses note he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes.

The witnesses note the figures of Judah's rich plenty — vines so abundant one might bind an ass to them, wine as common as washing-water — and the Messianic glance, that "the blood of grapes" points to the blood of Christ, the true Vine who treads the winepress.

So the witnesses make the verse the figures of plenty and the glance at Christ's blood. The lesson the expositors draw is that the vine and wine figure the rich fruitfulness of Judah's land, and the blood of grapes points to the blood of Christ. The witnesses note the foal to the vine: "binding his foal unto the vine" — Gill notes vines in plenty; the foal to the vine; the the vine; the plenty; the large; the abundant. The witnesses press the washed in wine: "washed his garments in wine" — wine as common as water; the washed in wine; the as water; the abundance. The expositors draw the lesson on the blood of grapes: "the blood of grapes" — Christ's blood; the blood of grapes; the the blood; the of grapes; the Christ; the the winepress. The expositors note the fit for both: fit for tribe and Son; the fit for both; the the richness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he washed his garments in wine and his clothes in the blood of grapes, and the plenty. The verse thus records the figures of plenty — teaching that the vine and wine figure Judah's fruitfulness, and the blood of grapes points to the blood of Christ.
12:
"His eyes shall be red with wine, and his teeth white with milk." The witnesses note the figures of plenty completed, the eyes red with wine and teeth white with milk. Gill notes this signifies not the intemperance of the tribe but the goodness and generosity of their wine, and (applied to Messiah) may denote the joy that sparkled in his eyes. The witnesses note the eyes red with wine and the teeth white with milk; the closing figure of Judah's abundance of wine and milk, with a Messianic glance — that Jacob completes the picture of plenty: "His eyes shall be red with wine, and his teeth white with milk"; the witnesses note the meaning, not the "intemperance of this tribe" but "the goodness and generosity of their wine," that Judah's land should so abound in wine and milk that the people should be ruddy and well-favoured, the eyes bright ("red") with the richness of wine and the teeth "white with milk," a picture of health, plenty, and prosperity; the witnesses note that wine and milk together figure the fulness of the land, the vineyards and the pastures both yielding abundantly; the witnesses note the Messianic application the commentators offer, that as applied to Christ, the antitype of Judah, the figures may denote his comeliness, or the joy and the saving fulness found in him, who gives the wine and milk of the gospel freely (cf. Isa. 55:1); the witnesses note the richness of blessing that closes Judah's portion; so that Judah's plenty of wine and milk completes the blessing; and his teeth white with milk. The witnesses note His eyes shall be red with wine. The witnesses note and his teeth white with milk.

The witnesses note the closing figure of Judah's abundance — eyes red with wine, teeth white with milk — a picture of health and plenty, the vineyards and pastures yielding richly; with a Messianic glance at the saving fulness found in Christ, who gives the wine and milk of the gospel freely.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing figure of Judah's abundance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the wine and milk figure the fulness and prosperity of Judah's land, with a glance at the saving fulness in Christ. The witnesses note the eyes red with wine: "His eyes shall be red with wine" — Gill notes the goodness of the wine; the eyes red with wine; the the goodness; the the wine; the not intemperance; the ruddy. The expositors observe the teeth white with milk: "his teeth white with milk" — health and plenty; the teeth white with milk; the the milk; the health; the plenty; the well-favoured. The witnesses press the wine and milk: wine and milk, the fulness of the land; the wine and milk; the wine; the milk; the the fulness; the the land. The expositors draw the lesson on the vineyards and pastures: the vineyards and pastures yielding; the vineyards and pastures; the vineyards; the pastures; the yielding; the abundantly. The witnesses observe the saving fulness: the saving fulness in Christ; the saving fulness; the saving; the fulness; the Christ; the the gospel. The expositors note the freely given: the wine and milk freely given; the freely given; the freely; the given; the the gospel; the Isaiah. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the His eyes shall be red with wine, the and his teeth white with milk, and the abundance. The verse thus records the closing figure of Judah's abundance — teaching that the wine and milk figure the fulness of the land, with a glance at the saving fulness in Christ.
